info.title: The Art of the Model Flow
info.subtitle: On the Transformations of Form Without Loss of Meaning

abstract.para: The master programmer said: Water takes the shape of its vessel yet never loses its substance. Ice, vapor, stream, rain — the molecule persists through every transformation. So too the document model. From serialized XML to object graph, from object graph to mirror representation, from mirror to JSON, from JSON to the living DOM — each transformation preserves the whole. This is the Model Flow: a chain of explicit models and deterministic transformers through which a document passes without losing a single atom of meaning.

para.quote: The generals who understand war prevail; those who do not, perish.

section.title: Why the Model Flow Matters
section.para: A document is not its syntax. A DocBook document is not the angle brackets that enclose it, nor the indentation that formats it, nor the file system path that stores it. The document is a structure of meaning — chapters that contain sections, paragraphs that contain emphasis, tables that organize knowledge into rows and columns.

The challenge of document engineering is this: how do we carry that structure through multiple representations without corruption? How do we transform from one form to another, each serving a different purpose, and arrive at the end with the same meaning we began with?

The answer is the Model Flow: a precisely defined chain where each link is a typed model with unambiguous semantics, and each transition is a deterministic transformer with a defined inverse. The chain is:

listitem.para: XML text — the serialized, interoperable form
Lutaml model objects — the parsed, typed Ruby object graph
DocbookMirror nodes — the presentation-oriented intermediate representation
Mirror JSON — the serialized intermediate, ready for any consumer
HTML DOM — the rendered, interactive form in the browser

section.para: Like water passing from glacier to river to cloud to rain, the substance remains H₂O. The document remains a document.

section.title: The Principle of Reversible Transformation
section.para: The ancients said: What can be assembled can be disassembled; what can be disassembled can be reassembled. This is the test of a true model flow: every transformer must be invertible. If you can go from XML to object to mirror to JSON, you must be able to return from JSON to mirror to object to XML without loss.

This is not a theoretical aspiration. It is an engineering requirement. A transformer that discards information is not a transformer — it is a filter. Filters have their place, but a model flow demands transformers that preserve the complete semantic content of their input.

Consider the calligrapher who copies a poem. If the copy omits a single character, the meaning is corrupted. The model flow must copy every character, every emphasis, every cross-reference, every footnote — transforming the form but never the substance.

para.quote: In the practical art of war, the best thing of all is to take the enemy's country whole and intact.

section.title: The Five Layers
section.para: The model flow is organized as five layers, each with a distinct model type and a pair of transformers (forward and reverse):

table.title: The Five Layers of the Model Flow

section.para: Each layer depends only on its immediate neighbor. The XML parser knows nothing of the mirror. The mirror transformer knows nothing of JSON. The JSON serializer knows nothing of the DOM. This separation is not accidental — it is the source of the system's strength.

The principle of separation: When each layer sees only its neighbor, a change in one layer cannot corrupt another. The calligrapher need not understand the paper-maker's craft; both produce work that the other can use.

section.title: The Flow Diagram
section.para: The model flow proceeds in one direction for rendering, and can proceed in the reverse for round-tripping:
section.programlisting:
FORWARD (Rendering):
XML ──parse──▶ Elements ──transform──▶ Mirror ──serialize──▶ JSON ──render──▶ DOM String Objects Nodes String HTML
REVERSE (Round-trip):
DOM ──extract──▶ JSON ──parse──▶ Mirror ──rebuild──▶ Elements ──emit──▶ XML HTML String Nodes Objects String
section.para: Both directions traverse the same models. The transformers are different, but the models are shared. This is what makes round-trip fidelity possible: the same types serve as the contract between every pair of transformers.

para.quote: Know the terrain and know yourself; in a hundred battles you will never be in peril.

section.title: The XML Model: Form as Syntax
section.para: XML is the outer garment of the document. It is the form in which documents travel between systems, are stored in repositories, and are exchanged between organizations. XML is universal but inert — it is text, not behavior.

In the model flow, XML is the serialization layer. It serves one purpose: to encode the document as a stream of characters that any conforming parser can reconstruct. The encoding is well-defined by the DocBook 5 schema, which constrains which elements may appear where and what attributes they carry.

A DocBook XML document names its elements by semantic role — <chapter>, <section>, <para>, <emphasis>. These names are the contract between the author and the system. They carry meaning that survives every transformation in the chain.

para.emphasis: The bamboo scroll carries the poem; the poem is not the scroll.

section.title: The Element Model: Form as Objects
section.para: When XML text enters the Lutaml parser, it becomes a graph of typed Ruby objects. Each DocBook element — <book>, <chapter>, <para> — is instantiated as a class in the Docbook::Elements namespace.
These element classes are not passive data structures. They are active models with:

listitem.para: Typed attributes — a title is a Title object, not a string; an xml_id is an XmlIdType, not untyped text
Collections — a chapter holds zero or more Section objects, automatically accumulated from multiple XML elements
Mixed content — a para contains an interleaving of text strings and inline elements (emphasis, links, code), traversable via each_mixed_content
Round-trip guarantee — every element can serialize back to XML via to_xml, producing output that re-parses to the same object graph

section.para: The element model is the canonical representation. It is the form in which the document's semantics are most directly accessible to program logic. Every other form in the chain is derived from this one, or can reproduce it.

para.emphasis: The sculptor sees the figure within the stone; the element model sees the document within the XML.

section.title: The Mirror Model: Form as Nodes and Marks
section.para: The DocbookMirror is a presentation-oriented intermediate representation inspired by the ProseMirror editor model. Where the element model organizes content by DocBook semantics (chapters, sections, paragraphs), the mirror model organizes content by rendering role (block nodes, inline text, marks for styling).
The mirror has two kinds of primitives:

listitem.para: Block-level structures that form the document tree. A Mirror::Node::Document contains Mirror::Node::Section nodes, which contain Mirror::Node::Paragraph nodes, which contain Mirror::Node::Text leaves. Each node has a PM_TYPE — a string constant that identifies its kind: "chapter", "paragraph", "code_block", "table", "image", and so on.

Inline annotations that decorate text without altering the tree structure. A Mirror::Mark::Emphasis wraps text in italic meaning; a Mirror::Mark::Code wraps it in monospace; a Mirror::Mark::Link binds it to a URL. Marks compose freely — a text node can carry both emphasis and a link simultaneously.

section.para: The mirror model is not a simplification of the element model. It is a re-perspective. The same information, viewed from the angle of rendering rather than the angle of authoring. Every DocBook element that has a visual representation maps to exactly one mirror node type or one mark type.

para.emphasis: The mountain appears different from east and west; it is the same mountain.

section.title: The JSON Model: Form as Transport
section.para: JSON is the lingua franca of the web. When the mirror model is serialized to JSON, it becomes a data structure that any programming language can consume: JavaScript in the browser, Python in a notebook, Rust in a build pipeline.
The JSON format is dictated by the mirror's structure:

Each node becomes a JSON object with "type", "attrs", and "content". Each text fragment carries its text and an optional array of marks. The structure is self-describing — a consumer need not read a schema to understand it.
The JSON model is the transport layer. It is the form in which the document crosses the boundary from server to client, from Ruby to JavaScript, from build pipeline to browser.

para.emphasis: The river carries the mountain's reflection to the valley below; the reflection is faithful, though the medium has changed.

section.title: The DOM Model: Form as Living Interface
section.para:
At the end of the forward chain, the mirror JSON arrives in the browser and is rendered by Vue components into the HTML DOM. The MirrorRenderer walks the node tree and emits the appropriate HTML for each type — a
for sections, a

for paragraphs, a for inline code, a for tables.

The DOM is not the terminus — it is the living form. Here the document becomes interactive: themes change its colors, search indexes its text, the sidebar maps its structure, and the reader turns its pages.

Crucially, the DOM rendering is derived entirely from the mirror JSON. There is no hidden state, no server dependency, no auxiliary data source. The JSON is the document, and the DOM is its projection. A single self-contained HTML file carries everything — styles, scripts, data — and works offline from the local filesystem.

para.emphasis: The water in the cup takes the shape of the cup; the document in the browser takes the shape of the reader.

para.quote: The skillful fighter puts himself beyond defeat and waits for the opportunity to defeat the enemy.

section.title: The Forward Path: From XML to DOM
section.para: The forward transformation proceeds in four stages, each stage consuming the output of the previous:

listitem.para: Parse. The Lutaml XML parser reads the XML string and constructs the element object graph. Each XML element becomes a typed Ruby object. Each attribute becomes a typed property. Mixed content is preserved through each_mixed_content.

Transform. Mirror::Transformer.from_docbook walks the element tree. For each structural element (chapter, section, paragraph), it produces a Mirror::Node. For each inline element (emphasis, code, link), it produces a Mirror::Mark on a text node. The result is a Mirror::Node::Document containing the full node tree.
Serialize. The mirror tree is converted to a JSON string via to_h (which produces a plain Hash) and JSON.generate (which produces a string). The JSON is embedded in the HTML as window.DOCBOOK_DATA.
Render. The Vue application reads window.DOCBOOK_DATA, stores it in Pinia, and the MirrorRenderer component walks the node tree to produce the interactive HTML reader.

section.para: Four transformers, four forms, one document. The substance never changes.

section.title: The Reverse Path: From Mirror to XML
section.para: The reverse transformation — from mirror nodes back to DocBook elements — is the proof of the model flow's integrity. If the reverse produces the same semantics as the original, the chain is faithful.

Mirror::Transformer.to_docbook performs this reverse walk: mirror nodes become element objects, text nodes with marks become mixed-content paragraphs, and the resulting element tree serializes to XML.
The round-trip test exercises this path:
section.programlisting: # The complete round-trip xml_string = File.read("book.xml") elements = Docbook::Document.from_xml(xml_string) # XML → Elements mirror = Mirror::Transformer.from_docbook(elements) # Elements → Mirror recovered = Mirror::Transformer.to_docbook(mirror) # Mirror → Elements output_xml = recovered.to_xml # Elements → XML # output_xml parses to the same semantic structure as xml_string
section.para: Not every detail is preserved character-for-character — whitespace may differ, attribute order may change. But semantic equivalence is maintained: the same chapters, the same sections, the same emphasis, the same cross-references.

para.emphasis: The potter's wheel reshapes the clay, but the clay is the same clay.

section.title: On XIncludes and Cross-References
section.para: Before parsing, the model flow must handle a practical concern: real DocBook documents are often split across many files, joined by <xi:include> elements. The Docbook::XIncludeResolver resolves these includes before parsing, producing a single unified XML document that the parser can consume.

After parsing, cross-references — <xref> elements with linkend attributes — must be resolved to their target titles. The Docbook::XrefResolver builds an xml:id lookup map by traversing the element tree, enabling O(1) resolution of any reference.
These are not part of the core model flow but are essential pre-processing and post-processing stages that prepare the document for the transformation chain.

para.emphasis: Before the battle, the general must gather intelligence. Before the flow, the resolver must gather includes.

para.quote: Let your plans be dark and impenetrable as night, and when you move, fall like a thunderbolt.

section.title: The SinglePage Pipeline
section.para: The Docbook::Output::SinglePage class orchestrates the complete forward model flow. Given an XML file path, it:

listitem.para: Resolves XIncludes — flattening multi-file documents into a single XML tree
Parses to elements — building the typed object graph via Document.from_xml
Generates a table of contents — extracting the hierarchical section structure as a navigable tree
Generates section numbering — computing hierarchical numbers (1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, 2.1) for each section
Collects index terms — gathering <indexterm> elements into an organized index
Transforms to mirror — converting the element tree to DocbookMirror nodes and marks
Resolves image paths — converting relative fileref paths to embedded data URLs or absolute references
Assembles the HTML — inlining CSS, JavaScript, and the mirror JSON into a single self-contained file

section.para: The output is one HTML file. Open it in any browser, from any filesystem, with no server. The document lives.

section.title: On Fidelity
section.para: The measure of the model flow is fidelity: does the final output convey the same meaning as the original input?

Fidelity is not a binary — it is a spectrum. At one end, the raw XML string (perfect fidelity, no utility). At the other, a plain-text extraction (maximum utility, zero fidelity). The model flow occupies the optimal point: full semantic fidelity with maximum presentation utility.
Consider what is preserved at each layer:

table.title: What Is Preserved at Each Layer

row.entry: Element names, attributes, text, mixed content, xml:id
Semantic structure, text content, inline styling, links
All node types, all marks, all attributes
All content, all structure, all styling

section.para: At no point is content discarded. At no point is structure flattened. The document that emerges from the chain carries every atom of meaning that entered it.

para.emphasis: The master painter's copy is indistinguishable from the original because both capture the same truth.

para.quote: In the midst of chaos, there is also opportunity.

section.title: Extending the Model Flow
section.para: The model flow is designed for extension. Adding a new DocBook element requires adding an element class, a mirror node type, and a case in the transformer — three points of change, each in its own layer. The layers do not entangle; they compose.
New output formats follow the same pattern. Today the chain produces HTML. Tomorrow it could produce:

listitem.para: PDF — via a mirror-to-PDF transformer that walks the node tree and emits typeset pages
EPUB — via a mirror-to-EPUB transformer that packages the content as a reflowable ebook
Editor state — by loading the mirror JSON into a ProseMirror editor, enabling WYSIWYG editing with round-trip back to DocBook XML
Search index — by extracting text from the mirror nodes into a structured index optimized for full-text search

section.para: Each new output requires only a new transformer from the mirror model. The element model, the XML parser, and the forward chain up to the mirror remain untouched.

para.emphasis: Dig the canal once, and every field benefits from the water.

section.title: The Model Flow Principle
section.para: Let us state the principle plainly:

blockquote.para: The Model Flow Principle: A document processing system achieves round-trip fidelity when every transformation in its pipeline is governed by explicit, typed models connected by deterministic, invertible transformers.

section.para: The implications are clear:

listitem.para: Every form in the pipeline must have a defined model — not ad hoc hashes or untyped strings, but typed classes with declared attributes.
Every transition must be a deterministic function — given the same input, always producing the same output, with no hidden state or side effects.
Every transformer must be invertible — the reverse transformation recovers the original semantics.
Every layer must be independent — a change in one layer's implementation must not require changes in any other.

section.para: Violate any of these, and the flow breaks. The document loses an atom of meaning. The round-trip fails. The copy differs from the original.
Honor them, and the document flows like water — through XML, through objects, through mirrors, through JSON, through the DOM — always the same substance, always whole, always true.

para.emphasis: The greatest victory is the one that requires no battle. The greatest transformation is the one that preserves everything.
